Security

Tether is designed for trusted LAN environments. This page documents the security model, threat model, and best practices for safe deployment.

⚠️ Important: Tether is NOT designed for public internet exposure. Always place it behind a reverse proxy with TLS if you need remote access.

Security Layers

1. Authentication

AspectImplementation
AlgorithmArgon2id (memory-hard, PHC winner)
Salt16-byte random salt per hash
Memory Cost19,456 KiB (default Argon2id)
Iterations2 passes
Parallelism1 thread
Hash Length32 bytes

2. Session Management

AspectImplementation
Token FormatUUID v4 (128-bit random)
Expiry7 days from creation
Cookie FlagsHttpOnly, SameSite=Strict, Path=/
StorageIn-memory HashMap (no disk persistence)

3. Rate Limiting

AspectImplementation
Window10 minutes
Max Attempts5 per window per IP
LockoutBlocks further attempts until window expires
StorageIn-memory HashMap with timestamp tracking

4. Network Security

AspectDefaultWith --allow-lan
Bind Address127.0.0.1 (localhost)0.0.0.0 (all interfaces)
Port80808080 (configurable)
TLSNot enabledNot enabled

Threat Model

Threats Mitigated

  • Brute Force Attacks — Argon2id's memory hardness makes GPU/ASIC attacks expensive
  • Password Guessing — Rate limiting prevents automated dictionary attacks
  • Session Hijacking — HttpOnly cookies prevent XSS-based token theft
  • CSRF — SameSite=Strict prevents cross-site request forgery
  • Accidental Exposure — Default localhost binding prevents unintentional LAN exposure

Threats NOT Mitigated

  • Network Eavesdropping — No TLS; LAN traffic is unencrypted
  • Malicious LAN Actors — MITM attacks possible on untrusted networks
  • Shell Escape Attacks — User's shell inherits their user permissions
  • Denial of Service — No connection limits or resource quotas
🚨 Critical: Never expose Tether directly to the internet. It is designed for trusted LAN environments only.

Best Practices

Do

  • ✅ Use a strong, unique password (12+ characters, mixed case, numbers, symbols)
  • ✅ Only enable --allow-lan on trusted networks
  • ✅ Use a firewall to restrict access to the Tether port
  • ✅ Place behind a reverse proxy (nginx, caddy) with TLS for remote access
  • ✅ Monitor server logs for suspicious activity

Don't

  • ❌ Use weak or common passwords
  • ❌ Expose Tether directly to the internet
  • ❌ Run Tether as root (use your regular user account)
  • ❌ Leave Tether running on public WiFi
  • ❌ Share your password or session cookie

Production Deployment

For production or remote access, use a reverse proxy with TLS:

# Example: nginx with Let's Encrypt server { listen 443 ssl; server_name tether.example.com; ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/tether.example.com/fullchain.pem; ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/tether.example.com/privkey.pem; location / { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8080; proxy_http_version 1.1; proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade"; proxy_set_header Host $host; proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr; } }

Security Audit Checklist

  • Password is 12+ characters, unique
  • --allow-lan only on trusted networks
  • Firewall rules restrict access to known IPs
  • Not running as root
  • TLS enabled if accessible outside LAN
  • Logs monitored for suspicious activity

Reporting Vulnerabilities

If you discover a security vulnerability, please report it via GitHub Security Advisories. Do not open a public issue.
